title: Research Index
blurb: This directory contains research documents analyzing design decisions, exploring architectural trade-offs, and documenting reasoning for the Swift Institute ecosystem.

documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.7, 2026-05-17): Tier 3 ecosystem-wide design Research + IMPLEMENTED v1 for institute-native alternative to Apple's swift-argument-parser. 3-package v1 shape (post v1.0.7 scope reduction): swift-primitives/swift-argument-primitives (L1, 10 targets, 50 tests) + swift-standards/swift-ieee-1003 (L2, 5 targets, 21 tests) + swift-foundations/swift-arguments (L3, 7 targets, 47 tests). Total 118 tests passing; all Foundation-free; all lint-clean. Implemented via orchestrator/subagent pattern 2026-05-17/18 (3 sequential subagent dispatches). v1.0.7 scope reduction per principal direction 'we haven't needed this yet' applied [ECO-002] + [PLAT-ARCH-021] + [MOD-RENT]: dropped Command.Completion.{Bash,Zsh,Fish,PowerShell} (D10 v2 — separate L3 swift-shell-completion); dropped Command.Manpage (D11); dropped swift-gnu L2 (GNU long-options handled inline at L3). documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0, 2026-05-15): Tier 2 cross-package research closing direction D6 of 2026-05-15-swift-arguments-ecosystem-design.md. Recommends Option A single always-async Command.Protocol with mutating func run() async throws(Command.Error); no Command.Async.Protocol. Rationale: swift-argument-parser's sync/async split was Swift 5.5/5.6 toolchain-availability-driven (verified at git PR #404 commit 1141ed1, 2022-03-14), not structural; institute precedent (Parser.Protocol, Serializer.Protocol, Coder.Protocol) is unanimously single-shape; all 3 existing institute CLIs already use AsyncParsableCommand. Composes orthogonally with D8 ~Copyable Command.Resource.Protocol axis. Prior-art survey: optparse-applicative, clap, System.CommandLine Beta 4 single-surface collapse, Cobra, Click.

documents.topic: DECISION (v1.0.2, A1 implementation landed + A2 measurement gate PASSED): Tier 2 comparative analysis + implemented architecture for closing swift-json's 37% parse+decode gap to Foundation's JSONDecoder on schema-known partial-shape workloads. Predecessor: streaming-json-deserialize-status-quo-and-prior-art.md v1.0.0. v1.0.0 enumerated four candidate architectures; recommended Option B (event-emitting Span parser γ). v1.0.1 recorded A0 disposition: Premise 1 GREEN (Token.Kind storage incl .unknown(UInt8)), Premise 3 GREEN (withContiguousStorageIfAvailable), Premise 2 GREEN compile+correctness + RED §4.3 timing (Bar/Today=4.48× mock); implementation-side JSON.assemble short-circuit locked in as REQUIRED A1 constraint. v1.0.2 records A1 disposition: Wave 0 + Wave 1 landed across 6 commits in swift-rfc-8259 + swift-json (Span.EventStream cursor + JSON.Assemble with short-circuit + Serializable.deserialize(events:) protocol + foundational conformer event-grain methods + bench codable-lookup-event-grain mode + oracle migration + 55 new tests; 212 rfc-8259 tests + 47 swift-json tests passing). A2 measurement gate PASSED both axes DECISIVELY on canonical 86 MB Swift stdlib symbol-graph: axis (a) event-grain / Foundation = 0.357× (swift-json 2.80× FASTER than Foundation, vs ≤1.10× projected target — gap substantially reversed); axis (b) default-fallback 5.3% noise-floor delta vs status-quo init(jsonBytes:) (short-circuit working as designed). Wave 1 oracle migration: 0.149s release wall-clock = 6.4× release-to-release speedup; 136 refinement pairs byte-identical. Two acceptable deviations: peekStructural() helper added; consumeAsParseValue is public not SPI (SPI+@inlinable cross-module empirically REJECTED by Swift 6.3.2 compiler, captured in doc + commit f4f3cb2). §10 NEW captures full A1 disposition + three caveats: (a) simdjson framing intentionally DROPPED — theoretical not measured; (b) 0.357× is workload-specific to 67%-skip-ratio schema, low-skip workloads see narrower wedge or none; (c) Wave 2 (Lint.Manifest + Manifest.Load fanout) + Wave 3 (test infra) remain deferred per minimal-scope decision and [BENCH-010] / [RES-018]. Status upgraded RECOMMENDATION → DECISION at v1.0.2. Implementations field in frontmatter cites all 7 commits. Original v1.0.0 / v1.0.1 analysis preserved per [RES-008]; §9 / §10 record the A0 / A1 dispositions respectively.

RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0): Tier 2 descriptive characterization of how swift-json today performs the parse → typed-decode path (full-tree-then-extract) and the architectural shapes the JSON-decoding field uses for the same task. Motivated by parse-performance.md v1.2.1's measured 37% structural gap between swift-json's JSON.Serializable.init(jsonBytes:) and Foundation's JSONDecoder on the schema-known partial-shape decode path (canonical 86 MB Swift symbol-graph workload, Symbol schema reading 3 of ~9 keys per object). Status-quo characterization: swift-json's call graph (JSON.Serializable.init(jsonBytes:) → JSON.parse → RFC_8259.Decode → RFC_8259.Span.Parser → RFC_8259.Value → JSON wrapper → deserialize) fully materializes every byte before any consumer code runs; ~⅔ of work on canonical workload is immediately discarded. RFC_8259.Token enum exists at the public surface but is bypassed by the Phase A1 Span parser (toolchain-bug-driven). Tier 4 Span parser substrate is ~Copyable & ~Escapable over Span<UInt8> with lifetime annotations; mirrors Binary.Bytes.Input.View. The contracts a redesign must preserve: typed throws (JSON.Error), Foundation-freedom (production Sources/), ~Copyable cursor discipline, strict memory safety, public API stability, the 14× dynamic-access lookup advantage, JSON.Serializable as Codable alternative per codable-untyped-throws-disposition.md. Five-system prior-art survey (simdjson DOM vs ondemand, serde_json Visitor + IgnoredAny, jsoniter Iterator, Apple JSONDecoder + JSONScanner/JSONMap, System.Text.Json Utf8JsonReader, Newtonsoft JsonTextReader) with all claims verified against primary sources by parallel subagents per [RES-020]/[RES-021]. Pattern taxonomy: (α) full-tree DOM materialise-then-walk (status quo); (β) pre-built structural index + lazy decode (modern Foundation JSONDecoder since 2023 rewrite — exact precedent; simdjson ondemand); (γ) pull-driven event stream into target (serde_json, jsoniter, Utf8JsonReader, JsonTextReader). The field has matured past α for schema-known decode at scale; the substrate for either β or γ already exists in swift-rfc-8259 and swift-parser-primitives. Five academic citations: Langdale & Lemire 2019 (simdjson), Mison (Li et al. 2017), Sparser (Palkar et al. 2018), JSONSki (Jiang & Zhao ASPLOS 2022), JSR-173 (StAX). Outcome is descriptive — no prescription; Phase 2 produces the prescription via a separate /research-process invocation. Five loose ends classified per [RES-027] (2 premises, 3 directions).

RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.1): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide extraction of the cross-cutting structural finding from the just-closed swift-json v2 arc (value-tree-redesign-v2.md v1.1.0 SUPERSEDED-BY-EVIDENCE §12). The finding: Copyable value-wrappers + multi-buffer hash storage = refcount-per-copy cost dominates the algorithmic O(1) win at small N. The empirical record: the L1-1 prototype's storage swap from `[(String, Value)]` to `Dictionary<String, Value>.Ordered` regressed parse +339% and lookup +226% at the canonical workload (86 MB Swift.symbols.json, mean N=2.06) even though the isolated storage micro-bench (`crossover` mode) showed Dict.Ordered beating linear-scan 3.78×–192× at every N. A diagnostic Swift.Dictionary + side-order-array swap cut the regression roughly in half (parse +82%, lookup +159%) but still regressed materially — wrapper-indirection cost is dominant, storage-primitive choice is a 2× factor on top. The structural cost model: every wrapper copy increments one refcount per heap-backed component (1 for single-array, 2 for Swift.Dictionary + side array, 3-4 for Set.Ordered + Buffer.Linear + Hash.Table); a hot-path traversal of N wrappers with K heap-backed components pays N × K refcount operations. v1.0.1 softens three claims after first-reader review: (a) the integration-overstatement factor is scoped to the swift-json reference case rather than asserted as a general magnitude (the loose-end footnote always correctly classified it as a premise); (b) the N ≥ 30-50 break-even is relabelled back-of-envelope projection from §12's algebraic estimate — NOT empirically located, since the integrated bench was only run at the canonical workload's mean N=2.06 and never extended to large N; (c) Option B (break to ~Copyable wrapper) is clarified as theoretical-prediction-only — the v2 L1-1 prototype measured the *conditional*-Copyable L1 path, NOT the unconditional ~Copyable L2 path that Option B describes. The architectural fork stands: Option A (accept Copyable wrapper, keep single-array, accept O(n) — the swift-json default), Option B (break to ~Copyable wrapper, multi-buffer storage predicted-but-unmeasured), Option C (~Copyable companion in inline storage, e.g. Dictionary.Ordered.Small<N>). Decision rubric provided keyed on consumer's Copyable-requirement × median N. Methodological lesson promotable to a [BENCH-NNN] skill rule: isolated storage micro-benches MUST be paired with an integration probe through the consumer's actual access path before driving architecture decisions. Per [RES-019] internal grep: extends ecosystem-data-structures-inventory v1.0.0 + comparative-dictionary-primitives v1.0.0 + storage-primitives-comparative-analysis v1.1.0 + property-view-for-cow-copyable-types v2.0.0 + buffer-arena-conditional-copyable v1.1.0. Per [RES-021] contextualization step: cross-language analogues (Rust HashMap move-by-default; C++ std::unordered_map; Haskell Data.HashMap.Strict GHC-RTS-managed) do not exhibit the pattern because the wrapper-copy boundary that triggers it is Swift-specific. Per [RES-022] structural-correctness framing: refcount-cost model dominates over diff-size axis. Per [RES-023] empirical-claim verification at write time: every measured value cited against value-tree-redesign-v2.md file:line with verification date 2026-05-13.

documents.topic: Tier-2 RECOMMENDATION: how to add 'build my downstream consumers on PR' to swift-primitives CI for a 141-package, 9-tier ecosystem. Refines the 2026-05-05 prior-art survey's 'universal absence' finding: Rust's crater, Apple's swift-source-compat-suite, Haskell's Stackage, and GHC's head.hackage all replay the graph at toolchain level; the library-level pattern is hand-curated matrix jobs in upstream CI (tokio's test-hyper/test-quinn with [patch.crates-io], Vapor's test-providers matrix with swift package edit --path — both verified file:line). No off-the-shelf GitHub Action or SwiftPM-native feature exists. Recommends a new advisory reusable lint-downstream-impact.yml invoked from the universal swift-ci.yml (at the same chain level as lint-api-breakage, so 4-level workflow_call limit compatible), composed of: (a) cached dependency-graph manifest at swift-institute/.github with hourly cron + post-merge regeneration; (b) matrix-job reusable scoped to direct dependents by default with full-impact label opt-in for transitive closure; (c) class A/B/C/D classification mirroring lint-api-breakage with baseline-comparison discipline. Two extensions: nightly cron sweep for transitive coverage, and an optional swift-institute.org dashboard surface. [RES-018] premature-primitive cleared (two consumers: every per-package ci.yml + cron orchestrator). Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.

documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.1.0): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide policy research deriving an externally-cited best-practice for pairing (or not pairing) SE-0458 @safe / @unsafe attributes with SE-0302 @unchecked Sendable conformances. v1.1.0 adds a recency-verification pass against swiftlang/swift commits 2025-11 through 2026-05, post-SE-0458 evolution proposals (SE-0459–SE-0530), Apple-package main branches, and the official strict-memory-safety compiler tests; the v1.0.0 conclusion (peers, not partners — do NOT pair them) is CONFIRMED without amendment. Recency-pass additions: (1) refined annotation-position grammar — @unsafe has three syntactic positions (type decl / extension decl / conformance clause), only positions #1 and #2 ever coexist with @unchecked Sendable, position #3 is reserved for non-Sendable protocols; (2) UniqueBox commit-by-commit worked example (2026-01 through 2026-04) — the most recent live demonstration of '@safe type + @unchecked Sendable conformance' with @unsafe appearing nowhere on the type; (3) SE-0458 Future Direction on SerialExecutor verified NOT landed (no post-SE-0458 proposal amends the rule); (4) compiler-test dispositive evidence — test/Unsafe/unsafe_concurrency.swift:11 + test/Unsafe/unsafe-suppression.swift:77-83 codify 'class C: @unchecked Sendable' as the canonical strict-memory-safety pattern. Aggregate empirical count across stdlib + 15 Apple packages on 2026-05-13: several hundred @unchecked Sendable sites, ZERO @unsafe @unchecked Sendable pairs. Deterministic rule added: Sendable carries @unchecked when the compiler can't prove it; @unsafe lives on the unsafe declaration, never on the Sendable conformance.

documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0): Tier 2 cross-package survey of which types in swift-linter / swift-source-primitives / swift-foundations/swift-linter / swift-linter-rules / swift-institute-linter-rules / swift-parsers / swift-file-system should adopt ~Copyable under SE-0499's stdlib-protocol relaxation. Six-axis scoring (resource-correlation, size/hot-path, safety bug class, cascade cost, pattern-establishing, existing alignment) ranks Lint.Source.Parsed 25/30 as top pick (real per-file resource shape, large parsed-AST payload, pattern-establishing across all linter rules, mechanical 73-rule cascade, zero protocol losses since not Codable/Hashable today); Source.Manager 20/30 as second pick (single-registry semantic, large content payload, engine already passes inout, swiftc/Clang precedent); Lint.Run.Outcome 11/30 as profile-driven deferred. Confirms the brief's hypothesis: Source.Location-first (8/30) is the worst pick on cascade-to-payback ratio — 169 references, zero resource-correlation, Codable wire-format dependency in Test.Source.Location survives SE-0499. Already-~Copyable control set (File.Handle, File.Descriptor, Path_Primitives.Path, Path.Borrowed, File.Directory.Iterator) cited as canonical reference shape. Prior art per [RES-021]: Rust std::fs::File / rustc_span SourceFile / Linear Haskell / C++ unique_ptr; contextualization step argues for higher adopt/don't-adopt threshold than Rust-translation would suggest (Swift's per-type opt-in vs Rust's universal default). Adoption plan sketch for Lint.Source.Parsed: 6-8 hours single principal-authorization. Five open questions: 6.3-vs-6.4 gate (recommend 6.3+), visibility-method conversion, SwiftSyntax + ~Copyable storage interaction, Source.Manager bundling, @Sendable + borrowing closure-parameter composition. Per [RES-019] internal grep: extends noncopyable-ecosystem-state.md (DECISION) + se-0499-implications-for-equation-hash-comparison-primitives.md (RECOMMENDATION) + source-location-unification.md (DECISION, where the original 'Copyable cascade' constraint was the pre-SE-0499 framing this survey re-evaluates) + path-type-ecosystem-model.md (the L3-Copyable-wraps-L1-~Copyable reference architectural shape).

documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide audit extending the v1.2.0 noncopyable-adoption-targets-ecosystem-survey (linter / source-primitives scope) across ecosystem corners — file-system, kernel, IO, threads, process, sockets, source, parsers, lexer, parser-machine, binary-parser, storage, buffer, async, text, string, input, time. Surveys all currently-Copyable resource-bearing or large-value-typed candidates against the v1.2.0 six-axis scoring framework PLUS a new Wave-5+6 ecosystem-readiness lens classifying each candidate's cascade shape (closure-inference / inout-discipline / single-instance / stdlib-array-storage / stdlib-protocol / Codable-wire-format / cross-thread-shared). Adds Rows 11–18 to the predecessor's Rows 1–10. Principal finding: ZERO new top-tier candidates — the ecosystem has already adopted ~Copyable on essentially every type where structural shape allows. Wave 5+6's ~3× under-estimate came from TWO SPECIFIC absorbable-cascade shapes (closure-inference for the 73-rule Lint.Rule.findings cascade; inout-discipline for Source.Manager's call sites); the dominant residual Copyable surface is structurally gated. Highest-scoring structural candidates (recorded but NOT recommended without gating decision): Row 11 Parser.Machine.Compiled 14/30 (same shape as v1.2.0 Row 1 — reference-typed Cache in Copyable boundary — but blocked on Parser.Protocol ~Copyable relaxation across the parser stack, a Tier-3 decision); Row 13 File.Directory.Contents.Iterator 12/30 (strongest resource-correlation 5/5 + existing-alignment 5/5; IteratorHandle workaround at line 18 is a direct admission of the ~Copyable intent; blocked on upstream Swift Evolution ~Copyable IteratorProtocol/Sequence). Not-a-candidate rows for completeness: Row 16 Kernel.Thread.Handle.Reference (already a Copyable bridge over ~Copyable Kernel.Thread.Handle per the L3-Copyable-wraps-L1-~Copyable pattern); Row 17 sync primitives Kernel.Thread.{Semaphore,Synchronization,Gate,Barrier} (cross-thread-shared by design per [MEM-SAFE-024] Category C); Row 18 heap storage Storage.{Slab,Pool,Heap,Arena,Split} (ManagedBuffer-backed COW; companion ~Copyable Storage.{Inline,Pool.Inline,Arena.Inline} already exists). Deferred Row 14 Text.Line.Map 2/30 (stdlib-Array storage barrier + zero resource correlation) and Row 15 Source.Cache 1/30 (stdlib-Dictionary storage barrier + explicit value-type design choice). Generalization-or-accident verdict on Wave 5+6 calibration: SPECIFIC, not general — fresh calibration data is NOT a license to expand the recommendation set, must be re-verified per-candidate against the cascade-shape table. Already-~Copyable control set extended beyond v1.2.0's: Source.Manager (Wave 6), Process.Handle, Sockets.TCP.Connection, Kernel.Thread.Worker, Kernel.Event.{Source,Driver}, Kernel.Completion+Driver+Notification, Terminal.Mode.Raw.Token, IO.Completion.Entry, String+Borrowed, Lexer.Scanner, Input.Slice+Buffer+Protocols, Buffer.Slots+Ring.Inline+Small, Storage.Inline+Pool.Inline+Arena.Inline, Async.Channel.{Bounded,Unbounded,Take,Ends}+Mutex.Local+Waiter.Entry/Queue.Flagged/Resumption+Lifecycle.State.Shutdown, Parser.Machine.Builder, Binary.Bytes.Input.View+Machine.Builder. documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0): Tier 3 ecosystem-wide design Research resolving Thread H.3's class-(c) escalation (File.Path.Temporary.randomized adoption blocked on swift-rfc-4122 build failure). Recommends Option B: create new L3 unifier swift-uuids composing swift-rfc-4122 × swift-rfc-9562 × swift-random after repairing swift-rfc-4122 to drop forbidden L2-to-L2 lateral deps (darwin-standard / linux-standard / windows-32) per [ARCH-LAYER-001], remove stale Darwin_Primitives / Linux_Primitives / Windows_Primitives imports per project_platform_stack_l2_reclassification.md, and remove stale 'Linux Kernel Standard' product reference (decomposed to per-domain granular products). Mirrors canonical swift-random L3-unifier shape per [PLAT-ARCH-009]; keeps RFC_4122.UUID canonical at L2 per [API-NAME-003] (no name inversion). Four options enumerated: A minimal repair (REJECTED — abandons v4/v7 affordance, violates [ARCH-LAYER-011]); B swift-uuids L3 unifier (RECOMMENDED); C swift-uuids fully owns UUID (REJECTED — violates [API-NAME-003] + [PLAT-ARCH-018]); D per-version split (REJECTED — sprawl without [RES-018] second consumer). documents.topic: DECISION (v1.1.0): Tier 2 ecosystem-wide foundational re-examination of @safe-attribute usage and absorber-pattern invariant disclosure. Stamped Option B 2026-05-12: embrace SE-0458 @safe per language intent + layer mandatory institute invariant-disclosure requirement on top. Inverts [MEM-SAFE-025b] from forbid-@safe to admit-with-disclosure; introduces [MEM-SAFE-025c] (disclosure required when @safe present). Supersedes wave-4-absorber-pattern-policy-lean-2026-05-12.md. Empirical universe: 125 live @safe sites; three clusters (A direct ~70, B transitive ~30, C pure documentation ~25) all admitted under inverted rule. Structural rationale: @safe is the only language-level mechanism for argument-unsafety suppression and no-propagation guarantee per SE-0458; comments cannot fulfill these roles. Cross-language convention (Rust unsafe + // SAFETY:, Haskell Safe-Haskell + haddocks, PCC proof-witness + proof-obligation) is complementary attribute + comment. Migration ~20-40 invariant-disclosure additions; strict superset closure relative to Wave 4 carve-out (no sites lose @safe). Implementation pass dispatched.

Triggered by principal feedback on 2026-05-12-swift-spm-primitives-design.md (commit 14df127): no-abbreviations rule + maximal ecosystem adoption/reuse/integration. Framework is partly NEW codification (generic-noun-first framing) + partly making implicit institute patterns explicit (swift-foundations/swift-dependency-analysis already uses Package.* at L3; swift-manifest-primitives uses Manifest.* at L1). Carve-outs documented: typealias convenience post-v1.0.0; spec-mirroring acronyms RFC_4122/ISO_32000/IEEE_754; institute-grandfathered acronyms IO and similar. Cross-references existing rules: [API-NAME-001]/[001a]/[002]/[003]/[010]/[012]/[013], [PKG-NAME-001], [PRIM-NAME-001], [RES-018]. Worked examples: Version.Semantic not SemVer; Package.Name not SwiftPM.Package.Name. documents.topic: RECOMMENDATION (v1.0.0): Tier 2 cross-package companion to 2026-05-12-swift-spm-primitives-design.md addressing open question #1 (validation timing). Four options enumerated: A typealias (recommended), B validating struct (Glob.Pattern shape), C hybrid typealias + .validate() method, D typealias at v1.0.0 with future companion Strict struct in v1.1.0+. Cognitive-dimensions analysis per [RES-025]: Option A under-abstracts on error-proneness but matches institute consistency; Option B matches error-proneness but over-applies relative to upstream's discipline. DocC note SHOULD make permissive contract explicit so future readers don't assume the typed wrapper validates. Future-proofs an additive Strict wrapper if a v1.1.0+ consumer surfaces real construction-time enforcement need.

Substantiates the parent doc's deferral via cross-ecosystem prior-art survey and naming-convention analysis. Recommends Option C: separate swift-semver-primitives package owns SemVer.Version + adjacent types (Version.Range, Version.Set, Error); swift-spm-primitives v1.0.0 ships WITHOUT a Version type; v1.1.0+ MAY add SwiftPM-flavored dependency-requirement composition over SemVer.Version. Cross-ecosystem precedent unanimous (per subagent survey 2026-05-12): Rust semver crate, Node node-semver, Dart pub_semver all ship separate-from-PM. SwiftPM is the outlier — duplicate Version structs in PackageDescription DSL (Sources/Runtimes/PackageDescription/Version.swift:38-93) + TSCUtility (TSC's typed-throws variant) is direct empirical evidence that bundling failed in Swift. Community Swift SemVer libraries (mxcl/Version 336 stars; SwiftPackageIndex/SemanticVersion 70 stars; sersoft-gmbh/semver imports Foundation incompat with [PRIM-FOUND-001]; gwynne/swift-semver) exist precisely because SwiftPM's is not consumable outside the manifest DSL. No SE proposal codifies SemVer rules; SE-0152 references semver.org with patch-optional amendment. Per [API-NAME-003] spec-mirroring: namespace SemVer (matches semver.org short form), package swift-semver-primitives, type SemVer.Version. Three options enumerated: A inline in swift-spm-primitives (rejected — repeats SwiftPM's duplication mistake), B separate package (recommended core), C separate + explicit boundary that SwiftPM-flavored composition lives in swift-spm-primitives (recommended refined). This Research does NOT author swift-semver-primitives; recommends future Research+package-authoring dispatch when [RES-018] second-consumer verified at that dispatch's write time (candidates: Swift Package Index, release tooling, registry-aware tooling, NPM/Cargo bridges).

documents.topic: DECISION (v1.1.0): Should the cohort's functor surface admit ~Escapable arms? Verdict per package: (a) swift-either-primitives — extend non-closure methods (swapped, value(of:)) to ~Escapable arms TODAY with @_lifetime(copy either); defer closure-bearing methods (map/flatMap/fold) pending Gap A. (b) swift-pair-primitives — type-level ~Escapable upgrade required first; then non-closure methods extendable with same pattern. (c) swift-product-primitives — defer indefinitely; parameter packs don't admit each T: ~Copyable & ~Escapable. Empirical verification: swapped + ~Escapable arms WORKS with @_lifetime(copy either); map + closure on full ~Escapable arms FAILS (Gap A); mixed-arm map (one ~Escapable, one Copyable) WORKS. PairNE feasibility verified via /tmp/escapable-research probe target. Cross-references nonescapable-ecosystem-state.md (canonical state DECISION 2026-04-02 §6 rec 3) which already classifies Pair as a viable conditional-Escapable inline container.

DECISION (v1.1.0): Is there a non-method, non-init form for ~Copyable consume-extract from a property in current Swift? Verdict: YES via @_owned + consuming get + UnderscoreOwned, BUT structurally blocked for generic ~Copyable enums (Either's exact shape) on the latest 2026-05-07-a nightly post the 2026-04-30 fix wave. Non-generic ~Copyable types work. Phase 1 ships today: free function `value(of:)` for ~Copyable arms; property form `e.value` retained for Copyable arms. Phase 2 deferred indefinitely pending Swift compiler coverage of generic-enum consuming getters. Supersedes feature-flags-assessment.md §3.3 (UnderscoreOwned SKIP verdict) for the enum-payload-extraction use case.

DECISION (v1.0.0): Formalises the cohort's accepted disposition that Pair (struct) and Either (enum) ship @frozen + ~Copyable + no custom deinit, permanently fixing them as movement vehicles rather than resource owners. The combination forecloses adding a custom deinit in any post-1.0 minor version (deinit addition is an ABI break under library evolution); rescinding @frozen is itself an ABI break. Rationale: layout stability is a feature for primitive-tier consumers; resource-bearing types belong in resource-owning packages (swift-file-system, swift-process) where ~Copyable + deinit + no @frozen is the active pattern; consumers who need lifecycle behavior on contained values invoke apply with a closure that closes them. Asymmetry: Product is NOT @frozen — parameter-pack types lack stable layout commitment, so the annotation is deferred until parameter-pack ~Copyable / ~Escapable lands in Swift. Documentation actions for Item C: DocC + README must include 'Lifecycle: movement, not management' note; the Pair README's descriptor example must be reframed (or replaced with a non-resource example) to avoid the lifecycle-ambiguity flag the forums-review simulation predicted (forums-review-objections-2026-05-09.md item 3, score 40.62).

DECISION (v1.0.0): Formalises the cohort's accepted [API-ERR-006] violation on Codable conformances. Either and Pair carry synthesized Codable conditional on Codable constraints (#if !hasFeature(Embedded) gated); Product carries manual init(from:)/encode(to:) with explicit throws(any Swift.Error). All three inherit untyped throws from the Codable protocol contract — the institute's [API-ERR-006] forbids untyped throws on public API. With Foundation Integration excluded ecosystem-wide (per feedback_improve_ecosystem_over_foundation_or_thirdparty + feedback_ecosystem_no_foundation_in_main_targets), the standard Foundation Integration subtarget escape hatch is foreclosed; dropping Codable was considered and rejected (loss of ecosystem compatibility). Disposition: ship Codable in main target; accept the violation as a permanent contingent trade-off until a typed-throws Codable lands in stdlib (analogous to SE-0499's Equatable upgrade). The violation does not propagate — consumers who wrap the cohort's Codable in their own typed-throws-bearing API can re-typify locally; the cohort is the boundary at which untyped throws stop. Documentation actions for Item C: each package README MUST carry a 'Codable conformance and typed throws' sub-section explaining the trade-off; swift-product-primitives/Audits/audit.md CS-2 SHOULD cite this disposition note.

Decision rubric for sub-product splits in swift-primitives. Codifies priority order across three competing signals — upstream-pruning (Gate 1, dominant during pre-1.0 dev), structural axis [MOD-003] (Gate 2), capability rent [MOD-RENT] consumer criterion (Gate 3, PUBLIC-RELEASE only) — under [MOD-DOMAIN] coherence as Gate 0 hard precondition. Resolves the recurring misframing where sibling-variant split candidates were evaluated on downstream consumer count (the Gate 3 signal) in isolation, leading to proposals that passed conceptual cleanliness but failed upstream effect. Worked failure examples: Buffer Linear, Memory Pool, Async Channel — each passes Gate 0/2 but fails Gate 1 (no upstream-tree compression on extraction), so each stays as sibling target. Counter-example: LEB128 / algebra-primitives split — passes Gate 1 (tier compression from 8 to 3, freeing numeric-primitives consumer). Operationalizes upstream-pruning measurement by reference to the [MOD-025] uniquely-providing-dep audit. Lifecycle gating: consumer-criterion sub-rule of [MOD-RENT] is deferred from development to PUBLIC-RELEASE/TAG time per feedback_correctness_sole_driver_during_development. Three anti-patterns explicitly rejected: downstream-consumer-count framing as primary signal; conceptual-purity defense without upstream effect; file-count optimization mistaken for upstream-pruning. Decision procedure given as operational flow (Gate 0 → 1 → 2 → lifecycle check → optional Gate 3 at release).

documents.topic: Strategy for ecosystem-wide GitHub repository social preview cards across the swift-institute 130+ repo surface (Primitives L1 / Standards L2 / Foundations L3 / Components L4 / Institute org-of-orgs). Operationalizes the 'social preview images' future-surface enumerated in github-metadata-harmonization.md §13. Critical empirical [RES-023] finding: GitHub exposes NO public token-authenticated API for SETTING a repository's social preview image — confirmed by GitHub staff on Community Discussion #172072 (2025-09-04). REST PATCH /repos/{owner}/{repo} accepts no preview field; GraphQL Repository.openGraphImageUrl is query-only with no mutation; gh repo edit has no flag; README og:image is not honored as fallback for github.com URLs. The undocumented /upload/repository-images/ endpoint is session-cookie-authenticated (user_session — password-equivalent, not PAT-scoped), exploited by AnswerDotAI/gh-social-preview (Playwright) and drogers0/gh-image (browser cookie store) — both incompatible with feedback_no_gh_cli_admin_scope.md auth posture. Hard spec verified: 1280×640 px, PNG/JPG/GIF, <1 MB, 40 pt safe-area border (synthesized to 1200×540 centered safe-area to also survive 1.91:1 LinkedIn/FB center-crop), 48 px minimum effective font for thumbnail legibility. Prior art survey [RES-021] of 19 OSS repos: 4/19 (21%) ship custom cards (vapor/vapor + vapor/fluent — ecosystem template with glyph+wordmark slot; vercel/next.js — monogram + product tagline; supabase/supabase — wordmark + tagline + screenshot); 15/19 ship GitHub auto-generated defaults including ALL 15 surveyed Swift repos (apple/swift, swiftlang/swift-syntax, apple/swift-collections, apple/swift-numerics, apple/swift-foundation, apple/swift-nio, apple/swift-system, pointfreeco/swift-composable-architecture, pointfreeco/swift-dependencies, ChartsOrg/Charts, realm/SwiftLint, Quick/Quick) and rust-lang/rust, tokio-rs/tokio, stripe/stripe-node — strong precedent that custom cards are not table-stakes for research-grade infrastructure OSS. Four options analyzed: A=cookie-automation (REJECT — auth-posture fail), B=generation-in-CI + manual one-time per-repo upload (RECOMMENDED — matches Vapor pattern; chassis from existing tier avatars at swift-institute.org/avatars/; reusable workflow generate-social-preview.yml alongside sync-metadata.yml; commits .github/social-preview.png; nightly cron + lint-social-preview-staleness.yml drift detector), C=generation-only no-deployment (VIABLE fallback — degenerate form of B), D=no-customization Apple posture (DEFENSIBLE status quo — 15/15 Swift surveyed). Card composition: tier glyph (4-bar pyramid from existing avatar SVG, gradient bar in tier-correct position) left third + repo display name in heavy sans ≥72 px right two-thirds + 4 px tier-gradient bottom rule. Five open questions surfaced for implementing dispatch (background variant, display-name source, multi-target packages, cohort-tier mapping, institute-internal-repo coverage). Implementation gates per feedback_no_public_or_tag_without_explicit_yes.md: workflow authoring, chassis SVG, per-repo backfill all require explicit per-action authorization.

documents.topic: Pre-publishable evaluation of the cleanest possible consumer-facing syntax for the swift-linter ecosystem (engine `swift-foundations/swift-linter`, rule packs `swift-foundations/swift-linter-rules`, primitives `swift-primitives/swift-linter-primitives`) across the two consumer shapes that emerged via the architecture-cohort cleanup: (a) single-file `Lint.swift` declaring `let manifest: Lint.Manifest = …` evaluated by swift-manifest's subprocess loader; (b) nested SwiftPM `Lint/Package.swift` + `Lint/Sources/Lint/main.swift` whose executable IS the linter binary linking engine + rule packs. Premise correction per [RES-023]: the dispatching handoff's Current State described only the older init-kwargs Manifest shape; empirical state at HEAD 2026-05-07 shows TWO consumer-facing types coexisting by design — `Lint.Manifest` (typed `[Lint.Rule.ID]` via Tagged, JSON-serializable wire format) at L3 and `Lint.Configuration` (typed metatype DSL using `@Array<Lint.Rule.Configuration>.Builder` with `.enable(R.Type)` / `.disable(R.Type)` / `.override(R.Type, severity:)` factories, recursive parent inheritance via Ownership.Shared) at L1. Q1–Q6 sub-questions answered per dispatch brief: Q1a single-file Manifest = init-kwargs (current) + add `validate()` method; Q1b nested-package Configuration = current Array.Builder typed-metatype DSL, drop redundant `let manifest = Lint.Manifest(...)` declaration in PoC main.swift (eliminates current names-rule-twice defect with `if enabled.contains(R.id) { .enable(R.self) }` gating); Q1c cross-shape composition = Manifest is wire format + Configuration is in-process value + lift function for parent-chain hydration; Q2 rule authoring = struct + Lint.Rule.Protocol (status quo correct — macros add maintenance burden across rule packs without algebraic gain); Q3 per-rule parameters = pre-construct rule INSTANCE via new `.enable<R>(_ instance: R)` factory + optional `instance` field on Rule.Configuration (defer concrete migration until first parameterized rule lands per [IMPL-001] principled absence); Q4 severity/exclusions = status quo + v2/v3 path-typing alignment when Copyable typed-path L1 ships; Q5 parent-chain = wire format Manifest + lift via local rule registry, single mechanism across both shapes; Q6 citations = introduce `Lint.Citation` enum at L1 + split `static let message` into `static let citation: Lint.Citation` + `static let messageBody: Swift.String` (v2 before broad adoption, mechanical migration ~15 rules). v1/v2/v3 phasing locks forward shape; v1 changes are minimal (drop redundant Manifest in PoC, add Manifest.validate); v2 lands lift function + Citation split + instance-taking factory; v3 triggers when first parameterized rule or Copyable typed-path lands. Prior Art Survey [RES-021] across SwiftLint custom_rules YAML, swift-format `.swift-format` JSON, ESLint plugin packages, Rust Clippy + dylint, SwiftSyntax test harness, institute result-builder ecosystem — each surveyed with concrete syntax examples and what to learn / avoid. Theoretical grounding [RES-022]: wire-format vs object-graph distinction (Codable, Protobuf descriptor / typed-message, JSON Schema / typed-value precedents). Cognitive Dimensions empirical validation [RES-025]: all six dimensions favour the recommended shape.

documents.topic: Per-candidate analysis of splitting `swift-binary-primitives` sub-products into sibling L1 packages on the swift-binary-base-primitives precedent established earlier today (2026-05-07). Four candidates evaluated with both-sides argument per [RES-009]: (1) LEB128 → `swift-binary-leb128-primitives`; (2) Format → `swift-binary-formatter-primitives` (rename signal flagged); (3) Serializable → `swift-binary-serializer-primitives` (protocol-to-witness shape shift); (4) new `swift-binary-coder-primitives`. Empirical [RES-023] verification surfaced that `Binary.Coder<Output>` ALREADY EXISTS at swift-binary-parser-primitives/Sources/Binary Coder Primitives/ (plain witness conforming to canonical Coder.Protocol) — handoff's 'new package' framing is corrected to 'extraction candidate', and the architectural fit (decode signature is parameterized over Binary.Bytes.Input from parser substrate) makes extraction wrong. Verdicts: SPLIT LEB128 (second-consumer test passes via swift-binary-parser-primitives consuming Binary LEB128 Primitives as a Package.swift dep); HOLD Format/Formatter pending witness-shape resolution and upstream swift-format-primitives rename clarity; SPLIT-WITH-SHAPE-SHIFT Serializable→Serializer per canonical-witness-capability v1.2.0 DECISION + ascii-serialization-migration precedent (multi-phase, multi-org coordination, 9+ external consumers); DO NOT EXTRACT Coder (architectural fit wrong; existing placement correct). Property<Binary.Serialize, T> shape rejected for Serializer in favor of plain witness struct mirroring Binary.Coder<Output> — single-form operations don't warrant Property.View ceremony per [API-NAME-008]. No package creation in this session per the user's per-action authorization rule.

documents.topic: Reconciles `binary-base-n-encoding-family-architecture.md` v1.0.0 with two corrections that surfaced during Phase 2 execution: (1) `swift-ietf/swift-rfc-4648` already exists and is public — actively developed with `RFC_4648.Base{16,32,64}` types, RFC_4648.Base32.Hex / RFC_4648.Base64.URL sub-namespaces, String / [UInt8] extension inits per consumer ergonomics — the original doc's `[Verified: 2026-05-07] no rfc-4648 repo` claim was wrong (per [RES-023]); (2) user-clarified architectural principle: standards encode the spec literally and USE primitives as building blocks; primitives don't add to standards. Original Phase 2 had RFC alphabets as Property extensions on swift-binary-base-primitives's Encode tag — that inverts the dependency direction. Reconciliation applies the canonical institute three-tier pattern (swift-ascii-primitives L1 → swift-incits-4-1986 L2 → swift-ascii L3): swift-binary-base-primitives owns the closed-radix mechanism + parameterized algorithms; swift-rfc-4648 keeps its spec-literal RFC_4648.Base16/etc. surface and DELEGATES algorithm execution to the L1 primitive; the 'envisioned base public API' (`Binary.Base.`N`.encode(bytes)` with spec-default alphabets) lives in a future L3 unifier `swift-foundations/swift-binary-base` that composes L1 mechanism + L2 specs. documents.topic: Architecture for a coherent binary→text encoding family (base16, base32, base58, base62, base64, base85) replacing the legacy `swift-base62-primitives` (heritage drift from a renamed swift-standards package; mis-classified as tier 0 by the compute-tiers script when it actually has tier-14 deps; convention-mismatched namespace `Base62_Primitives`; only baseN package in the ecosystem). The radix axis is closed by encoding mathematics (~6 useful values capped by ~95 printable ASCII characters); the alphabet axis is open (RFC + de facto + custom). Recommends Option D: closed-radix `Binary.Base.`N`` nested types per [PLAT-ARCH-008k] precedent (`Windows.`32``) + `Property<Tag, Base>` from swift-property-primitives for call-site dispatch via `callAsFunction` (default-alphabet path) and per-Tag method extensions (variant alphabets). Aligns with [API-NAME-008] (multi-form operations under one root MUST use Property.View nested accessors) — `encode` is the root, alphabets are sub-operations. PHASE 2 SUPERSEDED by binary-base-n-rfc-4648-reconciliation.md (2026-05-07): the original's `[Verified] no rfc-4648 repo` claim was wrong; the canonical institute pattern (L1 primitives, L2 spec-literal delegating to L1, L3 unifier for envisioned public API) places the Property-extension public API in a future L3 unifier, not in L2 swift-rfc-4648. documents.topic: Recovers on-par-or-better performance for the Round-1 + Round-2 result-builder ecosystem when builder bodies contain `for` loops or bulk sequences. Empirical baseline (`Experiments/result-builder-perf/`) showed 11-39x slowdown vs imperative for `Swift.Array` for-loop bodies at N=100-1000. Root cause: per-iteration `[Element]` allocation in `buildExpression`, mandated by SE-0289's transform of `for` in builder body. Final decision (v2.0.0): ship Option G — bare `Swift.Sequence` overload added as `buildExpression<S: Sequence>(_:) where S.Element == Element`. An OVERLOAD, not a TYPE — satisfies the no-new-type constraint while delivering 0.13x of imperative for N=100 (8x faster) and 0.17x for N=1000 (6x faster). Combined with Option B (consume + append in buildPartialBlock), removes O(N) array-concat for mixed bodies. The `Repeat<S, Element>` helper (Option E) was prototyped, validated, and rejected in favor of Option G — identical performance, no new type, cleaner ergonomics. For-loop pattern documented as anti-pattern; users write `0..<N` directly.
documents.statusDetail: v2.1.0. The 'in-body .map is 21x slower' loose end filed in v2.0.0 has been investigated and REFUTED (see Experiments/result-builder-map-investigation/). The slowdown is not a builder anomaly — same cost inside or outside builder bodies. The v2.0.0 decision (Option G + Option B) stands. v1.0.0 recommended Option E (Repeat helper); v2.0.0 supersedes after the user constraint 'we'd prefer NOT adding a type in standard-library-extensions' + 'we control all packages, can change all code' produced the Option G + Option B path. Implementation shipped: standard-library-extensions builders (Array, ContiguousArray, ArraySlice, Set, Dictionary) + 13 institute Round-1/Round-2 builders. Empirical revalidation: 8/12 cases pass at <= 1.5x; the 4 failing cases are exclusively the explicit for-loop body shape (anti-pattern). v2.1.0 originally framed the residual as 'stdlib Collection.map's specialization gap'; that framing has since been further refuted in `stdlib-collection-map-actor-isolation-overhead.md` (2026-05-07) — the per-element cost is the runtime actor-isolation check mandated by SE-0423, conditioned on stdlib's .swiftinterface being built in Swift 5 mode, not a generic-specialization failure.

documents.topic: Refutes the 'stdlib Collection.map specialization gap' framing carried in result-builder-performance-optimization.md v2.1.0's Residual section. Empirical: V8 stdlib .map measures ~4318 ns/iter at N=100 vs V14 same-module @inlinable map at ~197 ns/iter when called from @MainActor top-level code in a Swift 6 SwiftPM target (~22x). When the same call is routed through a `nonisolated` function the gap vanishes (V8n ~202 ns, V14n ~202 ns). Single-file `swiftc -O` does not reproduce the gap (1.07x). SIL inspection: V8's specialized closure body emits per-element `swift_task_isCurrentExecutor` + `swift_task_reportUnexpectedExecutor`; V14's does not. Both have the closure literal fully specialized (`smul_with_overflow_Int64` for `$0 * 2`). Generic specialization is not failing. Mechanism: SE-0423 (Implemented Swift 6.0) mandates a runtime actor-isolation check at each invocation when an isolated synchronous closure is passed to a non-strict-concurrency module's API; stdlib's binary .swiftinterface is built with `-swift-version 5`, causing `isConcurrencyChecked()` to return false at the import boundary. Confirmed by harlanhaskins's closing comment on PR #82795 (closed without merge 2025-07-24). Recommendation (C): accept and document. Reject (A) report upstream — already known and tracked. Reject (B) ship `Collection.fastMap` workaround helpers — they would be a transition-period synonym for stdlib map that becomes a deprecation cost when stdlib's .swiftinterface migrates. Structural workaround: hoist hot HOF call sites out of @MainActor scope into `nonisolated` functions or methods. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide, DECISION.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0. Empirically refutes the v2.1.0 specialization-gap framing on three configurations of the experiment plus SIL inspection. documents.topic: Comprehensive security review of the swift-institute + swift-primitives CI/CD stack across 5 scopes: (1) per-component matrix of every workflow / composite action / helper script / cron orchestrator with action SHA-pin state, secret scope, network egress surface, supply-chain dependencies, and token-leak surface; (2) threat model enumerating six attack paths labeled plausible-vs-speculative — T1 secrets exfil via fork-PR (closed by GitHub fork-secret rules + no `pull_request_target`), T2 write access via compromised reusable (closed by permissions intersection rule + branch protection IFF landed), T3 swift-institute-bot 17-org App-token escalation (chokepoint = branch protection on swift-institute/.github), T4 supply-chain via unpinned action / curl-piped helper / docker pull (T4a Dependabot for GitHub Actions partial mitigation noted), T5 shell-injection via repo-controlled content (mitigated by env-var indirection except in audit-step pattern which reduces to T2/T3 chokepoint), T6 speculative paths (cache poisoning, container escape); (3) gap inventory — 10 gaps spanning correctness / secret-leak / supply-chain / write-access-elevation classes, with G1 branch-protection identified as the single highest-priority remediation (collapses T2/T3/T5 to one chokepoint), G10 bot-installation-permissions audit added as NEW Cohort F; (4) branch-protection minimal proposal with exact `gh api -X PUT` shape per repo and three-wave provisioning order (host repos → 4 canaries → 128-repo fleet) including post-PUT diff-verification step + L2/L3 wrapper-host forward-reference, explicit per-action authorization gates per [CI-052]; (5) cohort sequencing producing 6-phase plan with 13 per-action authorization gates total over ~3 weeks. Most consequential finding: branch protection on swift-institute/.github + swift-primitives/.github is the single architectural chokepoint that closes the bot's 17-org write-elevation path; without it, anyone with push access to those two repos can mint contents:write tokens on any of 17 orgs via generate-metadata.yml. Top-level verdict: stack is in good security posture for its maturity (post Cohorts A1 + B Phase 1); remaining risk is dominantly chokepoint-resolvable. No new ecosystem primitives recommended ([RES-018] check); every cohort composes with existing infrastructure. Composes additively with ci-centralization-strategy.md (Path B / @main strategy), ci-cd-prior-art-and-pattern-survey.md (3 high-value candidates — 2 landed, 1 queued), and ci-cd-cross-ecosystem-reuse.md (lint-org-bot-coverage.yml extension noted; L2/L3 layer-wrapper repos join Wave 1 in next protection refresh). Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.

documents.topic: Cross-ecosystem CI/CD re-use across the 17-org Swift Institute manifest. Six-question investigation, v1.1.0 with principal correction: (Q1) L2/L3 wrapper — MUST exist mirroring layer orgs (codified as [CI-004a]); v1.0.0's no-wrapper verdict reversed because the org hierarchy IS the architectural primitive and CI hierarchy mirrors it; create swift-standards/.github + swift-foundations/.github with thin-pass-through wrapper files initially. (Q2) 11 L2 spec-authority sub-orgs — 3-tier through LAYER wrapper (sub-org repos route via swift-standards or swift-foundations); per-authority wrappers DEFERRED, structurally blocked by GitHub Actions workflow_call 4-level depth limit (current L1 chain per-package→layer-wrapper→universal→advisory-linter is at the limit; adding sub-org wrapper would exceed); codify as [CI-004b] (sub-org wrappers are future work, sunsets when GitHub raises limit OR universal is refactored to inline advisory linters). documents.topic: Comparative prior-art survey of CI/CD patterns across 6 non-Swift ecosystems (Rust, Go, Python, JS/TS, multi-language large-OSS, plus Apple's swift_package_test reference). Cross-ecosystem synthesis identifies universal patterns (composite actions for setup recipes, scheduled-cron sweeps, harden-runner egress lockdown, OSSF Scorecard, OIDC keyless publishing), common patterns (workflow_call reuse, conclusion-aggregator job, hot-path/cold-path split, sharded testing, generated workflows from typed source), and convergent absences (layered package-tier organization, maintainer-operated cross-repo cron orchestrator, public/private visibility gate — all swift-institute-original design). Application to Swift Institute identifies 3 high-value candidate cohorts: (1) step-security/harden-runner egress lockdown (closes the supply-chain gap left by SHA-pinning declination); (2) Rust-style conclusion-aggregator job (decouples branch protection from workflow refactors); (3) OIDC keyless attestation for tagged releases via sigstore/cosign (establishes signing infrastructure ahead of SwiftPM Trusted Publishers). 3 deferred candidates (hot-path/cold-path split, swift-tools-version derivation, DockerHub→ghcr.io mirror) — analogs don't fit cleanly or trigger condition not yet present. 3 declined candidates (generated workflows from typed source, external CI, external dashboards) — appropriate at scales swift-institute hasn't reached. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.
documents.statusDetail: Survey produces 3 high-value candidate cohorts pending principal authorization for next-cohort sequencing

documents.topic: Tier-2 RECOMMENDATION: the L1/L2/L3 layering for the SwiftPM package domain. Surfaced by the swift-package-graph + swift-impact arc which audited the domain ownership of SwiftPM types. Establishes: (1) swift-package-primitives stays L1-generic (Package.Name/Product.Name/Target.Name typed identifiers only); (2) new L2 swift-spm-standard at swift-standards/ owns the SwiftPM PackageDescription DSL as Swift types — Package.Manifest, Package.Dependency (with .path/.url(Requirement)/.registry), Package.Requirement (.from/.upToNextMajor/.upToNextMinor/.range/.exact/.branch/.revision), Package.Identity (SE-0292), Manifest.Product (+ Kind/LibraryType), Manifest.Target (+ Kind), TargetDependency, SupportedPlatform, Platform — plus JSON Codable conformances matching `swift package dump-package` wire format; (3) L3 swift-package-manager foundation deferred per [RES-018] (only 1 direct consumer today; trigger is a mutation use case OR ≥2 direct consumers). Migration plan: Phase 0a author swift-spm-standard, Phase 0b refactor swift-package-primitives (remove migrated Package.Dependency types), Phase 0c update ecosystem consumers; Phase 1+2 swift-package-graph and swift-impact respectively. Pre-1.0 breaking changes acceptable per memory feedback_no_inter_launch_soak.md. Tier 2, ecosystem-wide.

documents.topic: Phase 1 deployment-readiness analysis for the three deterministic γ-class advisory reusable workflows specified in `centralized-swift-ci-and-spine-gate.md` v1.2.0 §3.4.2 / §3.4.3 / §3.4.5: γ-1a Foundation-family import enforcement, γ-1b License-header advisory (Stage 1 of three), γ-2 Mechanical hygiene (YAML lint + broken-symlink, separate reusables per §3.4.11). Originating dispatch (`HANDOFF-workflow-construction-phase-1.md` 2026-05-05) framed phase 1 as BUILD; on inspection, the four reusables (`lint-foundation-family-import.yml` + `lint-license-header.yml` + `lint-yaml.yml` + `lint-broken-symlink.yml`) had already landed (commits `ea4128f`, `6c3d726`, `2480628`), conformed to v1.3.0 §3.5 corrections (advisory-as-input pattern, ubuntu-latest no-container, shell:bash explicit), and were canaried green on swift-carrier-primitives + swift-tagged-primitives. Per supervisor block `ask:` clause, dispatch escalated and reframed AUDIT + TEST + DOCUMENT. Doc covers: (a) per-spec-clause conformance audit against v1.2.0 + v1.3.0 (every clause tabulated; all conformant with one-paragraph notes on each non-obvious match like the bare `#if canImport(Foundation*)` shape and the §3.4.5 'consolidated-issue vs separate-reusable' distinction); (b) empirical positive/negative scratch-repo verification — 8 fixtures (4 γ-classes × positive + negative) authored at /tmp/wf-phase1-scratch with a harness mirroring each workflow's gating shell; all 8/8 produce expected exit codes (γ-1a positive: 6 errors + 1 warning across 5 attribute permutations including @_exported/@preconcurrency/@_implementationOnly/package-import + Tests/Support ERROR + Tests/** WARNING; γ-1a negative: zero false-positives on `import Swift` / `import FoundationFooBar` / comment-imports / string-literals; γ-1b positive: 2 missing across no-header + MIT-header; γ-1b negative: zero false-positives on swiftlang-style + SPDX-Identifier headers + Tests/** + Package.swift + Package@*.swift exclusion; γ-2a yamllint catches indentation + line-length + dependabot indentation; γ-2b find -L detects absolute + relative dangling symlinks); (c) per-rule inventory mapping (γ-1a → [CI-022] + [PRIM-FOUND-001]; γ-1b → [GH-REPO-040]/[GH-REPO-041]; γ-2 → [CI-053]/[GH-REPO-060]/[CI-031]); (d) phase 1.5 deployment plan — clarification that phase 1.5 is NOT per-consumer caller fan-out (those exist already from B7c-B8d wave); the four reusables are auto-rolled-out via universal swift-ci.yml `[CI-031]` transitive ride; the actual phase 1.5 graduation is a one-line edit at universal swift-ci.yml's calling site (drop `with: { advisory: true }`) gated on 2 consecutive clean weekly cron sweeps + principal-side audit clean per §3.4.10; recommended wave order γ-2a → γ-2b → γ-1a → γ-1b stage 2 codemod → γ-1b stage 3 gate flip. Dispatch text mismatches: prescribed single `mechanical-hygiene.yml` (over-aggregated; existing two-file split is §3.4.11-conformant), prescribed SHA-pin all third-party actions (over-strict; existing `actions/checkout@v6` tag pin is [CI-013] + feedback_latest_versions_only.md conformant; SHA-pin reserved for niche actions per §3.4.5b precedent), prescribed `*-check.yml` filenames (existing `lint-*.yml` matches sibling convention). No workflow file modified; no remote pushed; existing implementation determined deployment-ready.

documents.topic: Sub-classification of the verification-taxonomy mechanical-bucket rules that apply to swift-primitives Swift code into four enforcement-tool buckets: swift-format / swiftlint-canonical / swiftlint-custom / custom-ci. Inputs read in full at write time: pilot + tier-1 + tier-2 + tier-3 classification docs (195 unique mechanical rules across 15 in-scope skills — code-surface 15 + primitives 4 + documentation 32 + existing-infrastructure 13 + swift-package 5 + swift-package-build 6 + conversions 18 + index 6 unique + memory-arithmetic 8 + memory-safety 27 + modularization 13 + platform 22 + swift-package-heritage 2 + testing 15 + testing-institute 9), swift-primitives root .swift-format (1907-byte JSON; 20 active swift-format rules + 14 disabled including ValidateDocumentationComments), canonical SwiftLint config at swift-standards/swift-standards/main/.swiftlint.yml (17 disabled + 9 opt-in + function_parameter_count tuning), Apple swift-format Rule Documentation (closed catalog of 43 rules: 18 linter + 25 formatter), SwiftLint Rule Directory (closed catalog of 324 rules: 129 default + 190 opt-in + 5 analyzer). Aggregate distribution: 1 swift-format (0.5%) + 1 swiftlint-canonical (0.5%) + 110 swiftlint-custom (56.4%) + 83 custom-ci (42.6%). Already-enforced count: 0 (the existing swift-primitives .swift-format active rules + canonical SwiftLint opt-in rules cover orthogonal formatting concerns; zero mechanical-bucket overlap with the in-scope set). Config-flips: 2 cheapest-path rules — [API-IMPL-005] One Type Per File via swiftlint-canonical one_declaration_per_file opt-in flip (single canonical-config edit; no fanout), [DOC-003] Method documentation via swift-format ValidateDocumentationComments flip from false to true (Fanout-Required: 133 sub-package .swift-format copies). Custom-rule-author cost characterization across 110 swiftlint-custom rules: ~25 one-liner regex (cheap, e.g., [API-ERR-001] typed-throws regex, [INFRA-020] Int(bitPattern:) trigger, [PRIM-FOUND-001] Foundation imports, [PLAT-ARCH-008c] L1 #if-os ban), ~55 moderate AST queries (e.g., [API-ERR-002] nested error types, [MEM-COPY-001] ~Copyable decl-site, [IDX-006] operator overload signatures), ~30 complex multi-decl / multi-rule clusters (e.g., [INFRA-200] operations-intentionally-missing closed pattern list, [CONV-003-006] API-table multi-pattern, [PKG-NAME-002/006] namespace+protocol+typealias triplets, [MEM-REF-002-005]/[MEM-LIFE-002-007] reference+lifetime primitive site enforcement). Custom-CI residual concentrated in: 27 Package.swift parsing (most of modularization + swift-package-build Linux family + [PRIM-ARCH-001/002] + nested-test-package rules), 30 DocC markdown / .docc file parsing (essentially all of documentation except [DOC-003] Swift doc-comments and [DOC-045] Swift // WORKAROUND: comments), 14 cross-package grep / cross-repo enumeration (most of platform PLAT-ARCH-* family), 5 build/shell invocations, 2 git-log/dependabot, 2 pre-commit hooks, 1 workflow-grep, 2 diff-aware predicates ([API-IMPL-010] visibility-widening trigger + [PKG-NAME-008] post-rename shadow scan). γ-roadmap migration candidates in scope: 1 — [PRIM-FOUND-001] No Foundation Imports = γ-1a deterministic in v1.2.0 §3.4.10, currently implemented as workflow YAML, could migrate to a one-liner SwiftLint custom rule in canonical .swiftlint.yml (parent_config: distribution removes per-package fanout cost); below the 5-rule cluster threshold, no coordinated migration dispatch warranted. γ-1b license-header is OUT OF SCOPE for this dispatch (readme skill excluded). No 5th-bucket (swiftsyntax-tool) candidates surfaced — every rule routed cleanly into the four buckets. Phasing recommendation (informational): Phase 1 config-flips (3 rules total counting γ-1a migration; minutes-to-hours), Phase 2 one-liner regex custom rules (~25 rules, low cost), Phase 3 moderate AST custom rules (~55 rules), Phase 4 complex multi-decl custom rules (~30 rules), Phase 5 custom-CI for Package.swift / cross-package / DocC / build-shape rules (~83 rules). Out of scope per supervisor block: any config file modification, PR drafts, workflow YAML drafts, γ-1a migration execution, fanout-mechanism work, generalization beyond swift-primitives, mechanical/hybrid/semantic re-classification.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0. Sub-classification dispatch closes the swift-primitives-scoped subset of the verification-taxonomy mechanical-bucket. 195 unique mechanical rules sub-classified into 4 enforcement-tool buckets with annotations (Already-Enforced / Config-Flip / Custom-Rule-Author / Fanout-Required / γ-Migration-Candidate / Tool-Alternative). Distribution heavily favors swiftlint-custom (56.4%) reflecting that most in-scope rules are AST/regex on Swift source; custom-ci (42.6%) concentrates in Package.swift parse, DocC markdown, cross-package grep, build/shell. Config-flip count 2 = strict-win immediate-coverage path. γ-roadmap migration candidate count 1 (PRIM-FOUND-001 = γ-1a Foundation, suspected migration to swiftlint-custom). No 5th bucket needed. No supervisor block ask: triggers fired (no irreconcilable conflicts; <5 γ candidates; ~200 in-scope total marginal-overage compliant per [SUPER-024]; fanout divergence out of dispatch scope). Implementation gated per supervisor block 'MUST NOT author or modify any config file' and 'MUST NOT draft any PR'; this RECOMMENDATION is the sole artifact. Generalization to other ecosystems (swift-foundations, swift-standards, etc.) is a future dispatch gated on this RECOMMENDATION's review.

documents.topic: Phase 1 rollout of the centralized linter harness for swift-primitives. Lands 3 mechanical rules: [DOC-003] ValidateDocumentationComments (swift-format catalog rule, fanout via new sync-swift-format.sh + swift-format-canonical.json) + [API-IMPL-005] one_declaration_per_file (SwiftLint built-in opt-in flip at Tier 1) + [PRIM-FOUND-001] Foundation-family imports (γ-1a workflow migration to Tier 2 SwiftLint custom rule pair). Architecture: 3-tier parent_config: chain — Tier 1 ecosystem-wide canonical at swift-institute/.github/.swiftlint.yml (NEW), Tier 2 swift-primitives org-specific canonical at swift-primitives/.github/.swiftlint.yml (NEW; parent_config Tier 1), Tier 3 per-sub-package overrides (132 files re-targeted from the no-longer-canonical swift-standards/swift-standards URL to the new Tier 2 URL). Apple swift-format has no remote inheritance so canonical .swift-format distributed via fanout (134 files updated by sync-swift-format.sh, mirroring sync-dependabot/sync-swift-settings family pattern). γ-1a migration: deleted lint-foundation-family-import.yml + lint-foundation-family-import-weekly.yml + audit-foundation-import.py + swift-ci.yml caller block + cron-audit-base.yml consumer-list comment reference. Peer CI/CD agent caught the weekly cron deletion (which I'd missed in the original draft); the weekly's cross-org drift-sweep purpose is absorbed by Tier 2 per-PR enforcement. Regex parity verified 24/24 cases (11 attribute permutations + 6 variants + 7 negatives) between deleted Python audit script and new Tier 2 SwiftLint custom rules. Two live user revisions during dispatch: (1) premise correction — swift-standards/swift-standards no longer canonical, re-derived to 3-tier chain at .github org-special repos; (2) source-refactor scope deferred — user explicitly OVERRODE supervisor block 'MUST fix every existing violation' with 'focus on CI; DO NOT exclude to fix; failing CI is expected; we welcome the violations as these point us to where we need to make changes.' Baseline violation counts (the intentional diagnostic surface): [DOC-003] 155 across 33 packages (swift-format linter; not auto-fixable), [API-IMPL-005] 23 across 6 packages (below supervisor codemod-threshold of 30; manual file splits in scope for Phase 2+), [PRIM-FOUND-001] 79 across 6 packages (top: swift-render-primitives 51 + swift-structured-queries-primitives 12 + swift-test-primitives 11; vs brief's expected zero — γ-1a was advisory not gating, so violations accumulated unenforced). Total = 257 violations expected to fail CI on first push wave; intentional. 5-wave push plan: W1 swift-institute/.github (commit 7f53280, 6 files), W2 swift-primitives/.github (commit 8a03db6, 1 file), W2.5 single-canary on swift-carrier or swift-tagged for live HTTP/cache/CDN verification, W3 132 sub-repo commits (uniform 'parent_config re-target + ValidateDocs flip'), W4 workspace-level root files (NOT a git repo; no push), W5 Research repo (this doc + index entry). Phase 2+ source-refactor cleanup is the follow-up dispatch chain.

documents.topic: Tier 2 swift-primitives-scoped enforcement-rule design grounded in empirical baseline of current Sources/. Maps user directive 'move away from bitPattern and unchecked inits, stay high typed, prefer map and retag' onto the SwiftLint Tier 1/Tier 2 canonical configs landed in Phase 1.5 / Tier-A. Empirical baseline (parallel-subagent grep, file-level counts, methodology per [RES-023]): Int(bitPattern: 180 files (mixed: bare form is the [INFRA-002] integration overload; chained .rawValue form is the anti-pattern); __unchecked: 41 files (mixed: declaration-site legitimate, call-site anti-pattern); .rawValue. chains 12 files; count - 1 34 files; Cardinal(0)/(1) 2 files (almost clean); Swift.min(...rawValue) 0 / count -= 1 0 / count &-= 1 0 / position.rawValue + 0 (already-clean discipline). Cohort design recommends 4 LAND rules (R1 cardinal_count_minus_one_anti_pattern regex `\bcount\s*-\s*1\b` warning ~50-100 hits cite [INFRA-025]; R2 cardinal_zero_one_constructor_anti_pattern regex `\bCardinal\(\s*[01]\s*\)` warning 2 hits cite [INFRA-101]; R3 chained_rawvalue_access_anti_pattern regex `\.rawValue\.\w+` warning 12 hits cite [INFRA-103]; R4 bitpattern_rawvalue_chain_anti_pattern regex `Int\(bitPattern:\s*[^)]*\.rawValue` warning ~30-80 hits cite [INFRA-002]/[CONV-016]) all at Tier 2 (primitives-specific, mirrors no_foundation_import_*/l1_no_platform_conditionals architectural placement; would be wrong at Tier 1 because Cardinal/Tagged-rawValue are primitives-specific types). 2 DEFER rules to SwiftSyntax-based tooling (R5 __unchecked: at call sites only — needs call-site-vs-decl-site AST distinction; R0 positive enforcement — needs conversion-tier classification per ai-context-reduction-via-type-system-tooling.md Option D Symbol-graph pipeline). 3 SKIP rules (R6 bare Int(bitPattern:) IS the canonical [INFRA-002] integration overload; R7 Swift.min(rawValue) and R8 position.rawValue + arithmetic both 0 hits already clean). Total expected diagnostic surface on landing: ~80-130 violations concentrated in swift-buffer-primitives, swift-tree-primitives, swift-ordinal-primitives, swift-hash-table-primitives. Failing CI is the intended diagnostic per user posture established Phase 1. R5/R0 deferral is the architectural retreat parallel to API-IMPL-005's Phase 1 retreat — both require AST-level context unavailable to NSRegularExpression. Composability with existing Tier 2 (no_foundation_import_*, l1_no_platform_conditionals): all five primitives-specific Tier 2 rules share reach (132 sub-packages via parent_config:) and severity model (warning + --strict CI gating).

documents.topic: Tier 2 ecosystem-wide prior-art survey (parallel-subagent verified per [RES-020]) of Swift Wasm CI strategy after γ-3's commit `8e3a76c` rollout surfaced a 6.3.0-SDK ↔ 6.3.1-compiler ABI mismatch. Six sub-questions: (Q1) SDK distribution model — swift.org canonical since Swift 6.2 (2025-09-15); every 6.2.x and 6.3.x patch has a Wasm SDK with checksum exposed via `https://www.swift.org/api/v1/install/releases.json`; no published guarantee but strong empirical pattern; community swiftwasm/swift-sdk-index has gaps (no 6.3.1) and is not a viable canonical source; swiftly does not yet handle Wasm SDKs. (Q2) Embedded vs Wasi disambiguation — three concepts: the `-enable-experimental-feature Embedded` compiler flag, the `_wasm` SDK ID (full stdlib + WASI), and the `_wasm-embedded` SDK ID. CRITICAL FINDING: both SDK IDs target the SAME `wasm32-unknown-wasip1` triple; embedded is NOT bare-metal; the `embedded-toolset.json` automatically passes `-enable-experimental-feature Embedded -static-stdlib -wmo -D__EMBEDDED_SWIFT__` to swiftc when `_wasm-embedded` is selected. The `_wasm-embedded` variant is the closer fit for swift-primitives' L1 freestanding-buildability invariant. (Q3) Stable-vs-nightly framing in v1.1.0 §3.4.6 was based on incomplete swift-syntax read — the actual swift-syntax Wasm matrix is `["nightly-main", "nightly-6.3", "6.2"]` (or 6.3 in newer reusable versions), both stable AND nightly together, hard-gated. 6 of 7 surveyed Apple packages (foundation/collections/numerics/system/async-algorithms/argument-parser) use the swiftlang/github-workflows reusable; swift-nio is the lone holdout with bespoke `wasm_swift_sdk.yml`. ALL hard-gated; only swift-collections uses the embedded variant alongside wasi. (Q4) Job structure — three options analyzed: α status quo + dynamic resolution (RECOMMENDED), β adopt swiftlang reusable as sub-job, γ full migration. Recommendation: Option α — replace hardcoded SDK URL+checksum with curl/python3 lookup against `releases.json` (sketch provided). Keeps two-job shape (`embedded` Linux nightly + `embedded-wasm-sdk`); do NOT add wasi-variant job at L1 (does not test L1 invariant). (Q5) Apple precedent — Wasm CI is now table stakes; dominant pattern is the swiftlang reusable; Institute starting advisory is more conservative than any Apple package; destination state should match industry hard-gating. (Q6) Soak taxonomy — extend class C with C.1 (install network/availability) vs C.2 (SDK ↔ toolchain ABI mismatch); §3.5.7's incident reclassifies B → C.2; if Option α is adopted, C.2 becomes empty by construction. Open follow-ups: per-L1-package empirical embedded-on-Wasm-WASI verification, L2/L3 layer wrappers' future Wasm decisions, swiftly Wasm-SDK support, Embedded compiler-flag stabilization.
documents.statusDetail: v1.0.0. Tier 2 prior-art survey produced via parallel-subagent verification per [RES-020] against primary sources: swift.org docs + API endpoints, swiftlang/swift-syntax + swiftlang/github-workflows + 7 Apple swift-* packages on GitHub, the official Wasm artifactbundle's info.json/swift-sdk.json/embedded-swift-sdk.json/toolset.json/embedded-toolset.json (extracted), forums.swift.org Wasm monthly digests March + April 2026 + the June 2025 announcement, Swift Evolution Embedded vision document. Recommendation: adopt dynamic SDK resolution from `releases.json` (single-commit edit to `swift-primitives/.github/.github/workflows/swift-ci.yml` `embedded-wasm-sdk` job's `Install Embedded Wasm SDK` step) — implementation gated per feedback_no_public_or_tag_without_explicit_yes. Keep `_wasm-embedded` SDK variant (matches L1 freestanding-buildability invariant; non-suffixed `_wasm` would not). Keep two-job shape; do not add wasi-variant at L1. Maintain γ-3 advisory through 4-week soak; flip to hard-gate to match Apple ecosystem default. Extend soak taxonomy with C.1/C.2 sub-class; recommend §3.5.7 incident be reclassified B → C.2 in centralized doc's next revision. No code change executed in this Phase 1 (research-only per parent handoff scope).

documents.topic: Tier 2 cross-package recommendation closing the L1 layer violation flagged in swift-async-primitives' 0.1.0 brief Phase 1 §#4. Investigation Q1 finding (verified 2026-05-04): the Kernel_Thread_Primitives fallback at Async.Mutex.swift:149-157 is unreachable. Four independent lines of evidence converge: (A) the precise module name Kernel_Thread_Primitives exists nowhere in the ecosystem — thread-mutex types DO exist at L2 (ISO_9945_Kernel_Thread, final class with manual lock/unlock) and L3 (Thread_Synchronization in swift-foundations/swift-threads, Kernel_Thread in swift-foundations/swift-kernel), but those are different module identifiers and incompatible API shapes (class with manual locking vs struct with withLock closure); (B) Synchronization is unconditionally available on every host that can build the package given Swift 6.3.1 toolchain floor + Swift 6 language mode + .v26 platforms — branch 2 covers all non-Apple, non-embedded targets; (C) zero callers, zero test coverage, undocumented in the package's DocC Semantics.md contract; (D) every in-package Async.Mutex consumer (Promise, Completion, Broadcast, Channel.Bounded, Channel.Unbounded, Publication, Semaphore via dependency, Waiter via Atomic) directly imports Synchronization separately for Atomic<T> state machines — the fallback is architecturally incoherent because a hypothetical Synchronization-less target would compile branch 3 and then fail every other consumer. Recommendation: Option 1 — single-commit deletion of lines 149-158 + cosmetic Kernel.Handoff.Cell doc-comment reference at Async.Publication.swift:63 + README §41 swift-kernel-primitives dep-list entry. Four rejected alternatives enumerated: Option 2 wire as real dep (creates a layer violation), Option 3 relocate to L3 (Q3+ path, not Q1's stop-condition), Option 4 leave alone (does not close brief §#4), Option 5 retarget at swift-threads/ISO_9945_Kernel_Thread (still a layer violation AND breaks the Mutex API surface — class-with-manual-locking vs struct-with-withLock). Stop-condition compliance per the handoff's Q1-vestigial branch: Q2/Q3/Q4 deliberately not investigated; the 4 L1 consumers (cache, pool, render, test) require no migration. Secondary observation tracked separately: surviving Darwin os_unfair_lock branch is platform-conditional code at L1 and would be a [PLAT-ARCH-008c] concern under strict reading; out of scope for Option 1 per handoff's secondary-concern framing. Execution remains gated per feedback_no_public_or_tag_without_explicit_yes.
documents.statusDetail: v1.1.0. Recommendation: Option 1 — single-commit deletion of the vestigial Kernel_Thread_Primitives branch (Async.Mutex.swift:149-158) + cosmetic Kernel.Handoff.Cell doc reference (Async.Publication.swift:63) + README §41 spurious dep entry. Bundleable as one commit. Closes brief #4 BLOCKED ON INVESTIGATION → RESOLVED. Q2/Q3/Q4 deliberately not investigated per stop-condition. No consumer migration required. v1.1.0 refinement (2026-05-04): Evidence A precise framing (the module identifier — not the concept — is what doesn't exist), Evidence D added (cross-file Synchronization-import invariant), Option 5 added and rejected (retargeting at swift-threads or ISO_9945_Kernel_Thread; both are layer violations AND API-incompatible). Secondary observation (surviving Darwin os_unfair_lock branch as [PLAT-ARCH-008c] candidate cleanup) tracked as separable follow-up; not bundled into 0.1.0 closure since [PLAT-ARCH-008c]'s strengthened reading was scoped to named types (Kernel.Descriptor, Kernel.Process.ID, Kernel.Directory.Entry), and Async.Mutex is a forward-looking ecosystem cleanup item rather than a 0.1.0 tag blocker. Execution awaits explicit principal authorization.

documents.topic: Tier 2 cross-package analysis (v1.3.0, status RECOMMENDATION restored after v1.2.0 false-negative deferral) of whether swift-equation-primitives, swift-hash-primitives, and swift-comparison-primitives can be deprecated once SE-0499 lands. The three packages fork Swift.Equatable/Hashable/Comparable specifically because the stdlib protocols required Self: Copyable; SE-0499 (Implemented Swift 6.4) relaxes that. Verification spike at `swift-institute/Experiments/se-0499-stdlib-noncopyable-protocol-conformance/` PASSES against Swift 6.4-dev DEVELOPMENT-SNAPSHOT-2026-03-16-a when the toolchain is selected via TOOLCHAINS=org.swift.64202603161a (env var with bundle identifier). The earlier `xcrun --toolchain '<snapshot-name>' swift build` invocation was a build-config defect — it sets the compiler binary but does NOT redirect SwiftPM's stdlib resolution; the false-negative diagnostics led v1.2.0 to incorrectly defer. The nightly's bundled stdlib `Swift.swiftmodule/.../arm64-apple-macos.swiftinterface` declares `public protocol Equatable : ~Copyable, ~Escapable` (verified by direct grep). Implementation truth: PR #85079 is the umbrella WIP, but the actual landings are split PRs by Ben Cohen — #85746 (Equatable, 2025-12-03), #85747 (Comparable), #85748 (Hashable), plus #85854/#85891 for ~Escapable. Checking only the umbrella PR is insufficient; for SE-0499 the merged-PR truth requires checking the split-PR family (verified against local `/Users/coen/Developer/swiftlang/swift` main, fetched 2026-05-03). Recommendation: Option C-refined — wrap existing fork protocols in `#if swift(<6.4)`, add `#else` branch with `extension Equation { public typealias \`Protocol\` = Swift.Equatable }` etc.; wrap each `+Swift.*` bridge body in `#if swift(<6.4)` (under 6.4 they duplicate stdlib's existing conditional conformances). Per-package surface preserved: hash-primitives keeps Hash.Value (Tagged<Hash, Int>); comparison-primitives keeps the three-way Comparison enum + Comparison.Compare + Comparison.Clamp + Property.View integrations — substantive value-type surface independent of the Comparable fork. equation-primitives sub-decision (full retirement vs transitional shim) deferred to a separate cycle; this cycle only adds the guards. Per-package guard application requires explicit per-package authorization per feedback_no_public_or_tag_without_explicit_yes. Supersedes the premise of Equation Primitives Design.md v2.1.0 §11.3 and §17.
documents.statusDetail: v1.3.0. Recommendation: Option C-refined — wrap fork protocols in #if swift(<6.4), add #else typealias to stdlib counterpart, wrap +Swift.* bridges in #if swift(<6.4). Verification spike CONFIRMED on Swift 6.4-dev nightly via TOOLCHAINS=org.swift.64202603161a. Per-package guard application authorized for execution by principal direction (do this proactively, test on swift 6.4) — but each per-package commit + push remains a per-action authorization per feedback_no_public_or_tag_without_explicit_yes; the action proceeds inside the workspace and stops at the push boundary unless re-authorized. v1.2.0 was a false-negative deferral due to xcrun --toolchain not redirecting SwiftPM's stdlib resolution; documented in changelog as lesson. documents.topic: Design choice for the public-init shape on Carrier (and Tagged via Carrier conformance) given the empirical evidence from the generic-throws-init experiment (swift-tagged-primitives/Experiments/generic-throws-init/, commits 2e0308c and 3686e7c). Two paths analyzed (path c — marker protocol — was rejected by user direction 2026-05-01): (a) keep the current __unchecked:-only discipline with consumers declaring per-domain inits via constrained extensions; (b) extend Carrier with V5 (default extension) or V6 (requirement + default impl) to provide a generic-throws init across all conformers. Decisive axis is validation-mandatory protection: V5/V6 add non-opt-out-able public surface (Swift offers no opt-out for default extensions or default-impl requirements); types whose semantics require validation-mandatory construction lose protection. Recommends path (a) on protection, reversibility, mid-launch-cohort discipline, and [RES-018] premature-primitive grounds.
documents.statusDetail: Recommendation: keep current __unchecked:-only discipline; no Carrier or Tagged source changes. Friction-reduction argument for V5/V6 is real but addresses only the wrapper-with-optional-validation case; consumers can handle this per-domain via V3-style overloaded extensions (the pattern the README already shows). Future revisit conditions documented (3+ packages re-deriving V3-style inits; a Swift language change lifting the H2 refutation; systematic __unchecked: misuse; validation-mandatory-type cases blocked from Carrier).
